selection.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?","opinion":"From my perspective, the idea of stereotype threat—the anxiety or performance drop that happens when you worry about confirming a negative stereotype about your group—sounds deeply unfair and frankly, exhausting. It's like carrying around an invisible backpack full of expectations nobody asked you to carry. When we talk about STEM fields, which are already incredibly complex and demanding, adding this layer of psychological pressure just makes the whole thing feel overwhelming. Given that I’ve spent my life in service jobs, dealing with people from all walks of life—and believe me, some moments are stressful enough without any academic expectations hanging over me—I can see how deeply personal these issues are. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: From my perspective, the idea of stereotype threat—the anxiety or performance drop that happens when you worry about confirming a negative stereotype about your group—sounds deeply unfair and frankly, exhausting. It's like carrying around an invisible backpack full of expectations nobody asked you to carry. When we talk about STEM fields, which are already incredibly complex and demanding, adding this layer of psychological pressure just makes the whole thing feel overwhelming. Given that I’ve spent my life in service jobs, dealing with people from all walks of life—and believe me, some moments are stressful enough without any academic expectations hanging over me—I can see how deeply personal these issues are. It feels less like a scientific problem and more like a social one that has been dressed up in academic jargon.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is highly curious (psychological feature), moderately open (openness score 66), very extraverted (score 81), but also quite high in neuroticism (score 84) and low in conscientiousness (score 13). The occupation (waiter/waitress) suggests practical, real-world experience dealing with diverse people under pressure. The tone should be empathetic, conversational, slightly dramatic (due to high neuroticism and extraversion), but also thoughtful and questioning (curiosity). The response must adopt a 'human' perspective that relates abstract academic concepts (stereotype threat) back to tangible, emotional, or social experiences (like service work stress).
response_parsed.tone: Empathetic, highly curious, conversational, slightly anxious/overwhelmed, grounded in real-world experience.
